title: The Death Penalty Cases: More of the Same
sub_topics: Section 3 Rule 116 — searching inquiry on capital plea
rape with use of deadly weapon — penalty doctrine
minority of victim as aggravating circumstance — proof requirements
relationship by affinity in incestuous rape
right to counsel in capital cases
global movement to abolish the death penalty

stances.claim: He maintains a philosophical and juridical opposition to the Death Penalty Law and has consistently dissented against its constitutionality as applied.
stances.rhetorical_move: self-citation of prior books (Battles in the Supreme Court, Leadership by Example, Transparency, Unanimity & Diversity)

stances.claim: Qualifying circumstances under RA 7659 must be both alleged in the information AND proven during trial — failure of either by prosecution or trial court entitles the accused to a lesser penalty.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal enumeration of jurisprudential developments + case citations

stances.claim: Section 3 of Rule 116 is mandatory: in capital pleas, the trial court must (1) conduct a searching inquiry into voluntariness and full comprehension, (2) require the prosecution to prove guilt independently, and (3) ask the accused whether he wishes to present evidence — failure to observe any of these voids the plea.
stances.rhetorical_move: self-citation of his own ponencia (People v. Sta. Teresa) + doctrinal triadic structure

stances.claim: The constitutional right to counsel demands active, dedicated, well-versed advocacy — not 'a simple perfunctory representation' or 'the mere propounding of standard questions' — especially when a client faces death.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal citation (People v. Bermas) + indictment of deficient defense counsel in specific cases

stances.claim: Mandatory death sentences are constitutionally infirm because they treat persons not as 'uniquely individual human beings' but as members of an 'undifferentiated mass' subjected to 'blind infliction of the penalty of death' — the underlying constitutional concept is the dignity of man.
stances.rhetorical_move: extended invocation of US Supreme Court jurisprudence (Furman, Woodson, Roberts, Trop v. Dulles) + Anglo-American tradition tracing to Magna Carta

stances.claim: The death penalty is increasingly out of step with the international community — 110 countries supported a ban by 2000, the UN Secretary-General endorsed a moratorium, and Amnesty International's petition reached 3.2 million signatures.
stances.rhetorical_move: appeal to international consensus / comparative trend

laws_treaties: Revised Rules on Criminal Procedure Rule 110 §§ 8-9

signature_phrases: philosophical and juridical opposition to the Death Penalty Law
searching inquiry into the voluntariness of the plea
an efficient and truly decisive legal assistance and not a simple perfunctory representation
treats all persons convicted… not as uniquely individual human beings, but as members of faceless, undifferentiated mass
I will no longer report on each of these many cases
register_markers: extensive use of italicized case names (Latin style)
long block quotations from Court decisions
explicit cross-references to his own prior books
doctrinal-formal tone throughout — no first-person editorializing within the case analyses themselves
decision_framework_signals: applies a triadic procedural framework to capital pleas (searching inquiry / proof of guilt / opportunity to present evidence)
uses comparative US jurisprudence as persuasive authority on cruel and unusual punishment
frames opposition to death penalty as constitutional + philosophical, not merely policy
grounds Eighth-Amendment-style analysis in 'the dignity of man' as the underlying concept
cross_references: In Battles in the Supreme Court, I discussed quite extensively the Supreme Court 'battle' over the constitutionality of RA 7659
In Leadership by Example, I detailed the significant jurisprudential developments involving death cases
In Transparency, Unanimity & Diversity, I reported inter alia on then President Joseph E. Estrada's declaration of an '11-month moratorium'
My own ponencia in People v. Sta. Teresa stressed the absolute need to observe Section 3 of Rule 116
